artifacts.content:
From: jordan.a.miller.hou@gmail.com
To: danielle.carter84@gmail.com
Subject: Long day + why I’ve been so quiet

Hey babe,

Finally sitting down after getting Maya to bed and packing her stuff for pre‑K tomorrow. I feel like my brain’s been running in five directions since last spring and I haven’t really slowed down enough to explain it to you.

You remember how things started to shift at First Horizon around June 2023 when the sales pressure ramped up and they had us on those back‑to‑back 10‑hour shifts at the Westheimer branch? That’s when the anxiety and panic episodes really kicked in. By the time I saw Dr. Samuel Ortiz at the Houston Methodist primary care clinic, he straight up said the schedule and quota push weren’t sustainable and put me on sertraline. He even suggested I look for a less intense environment unless they could move me off the senior personal banker role.

Then this year just added fuel. HR pulled me in on January 29, 2024 and told me they couldn’t keep letting me leave at 2:45 p.m. on Tuesdays for Maya’s 3:30 speech therapy with Dr. Alicia Nguyen at Texas Children’s in the Med Center unless they cut my hours. On top of that, after the teller override mess and the $4,800 check‑fraud loss, Lisa Ramirez handed me that written warning on March 18 at Westheimer and the regional basically hinted I should start looking elsewhere.

I’m trying to balance all of that with keeping up the Thursday 7 p.m. budgeting workshop at Wheatley High and our alternating weekends with Maya under the temporary family court order. No wonder I’m wiped.

I don’t want you to panic; I just need you to know why I’ve been so on edge. Let’s talk this weekend after Maya’s asleep and figure out what a realistic plan looks like.

Love you,
J

metadata.subject: Long day + why I’ve been so quiet

artifacts.content:
Title: Work + family stress log
Date: 2023-10-20

I need one place to dump everything that’s been piling up so I stop waking up at 3 a.m.

Work: Since March 2022 I’ve basically become the “heavy stuff” guy at the First Horizon Westheimer branch. Kevin Ramirez had me monitoring Mrs. Gloria Henderson’s account after Adult Protective Services started looking into her son for possible financial exploitation. That meant combing through her debit and wire activity in Fiserv every morning and documenting anything that looked off.

Same thing with Daniel Rodriguez after his Chapter 13 bankruptcy filing at the Harris County courthouse. From August 2021 through May 2022, every other Friday I’d sit with him to make sure his $425 biweekly trustee payment auto‑transfers were lined up and even temporarily lower his debit limits so he wouldn’t overdraft and blow up his plan.

Then there’s Malik’s Auto Detail on Montrose. Between November 2022 and March 2023, Kevin kept handing me those in‑person complaints after BSA flagged his repeated suspicious cash deposits and closed his business account. I had to explain three separate times why we filed a SAR and couldn’t reopen anything, all while trying not to take his anger personally.

Home: At the same time, I’m juggling Mom’s arthritis and PT, Erica’s nursing clinicals, and Maya’s schedule. In Spring 2023 I was color‑coding my calendar just to keep straight the Thursday 7 p.m. Wheatley High budgeting sessions, the 6:30 p.m. co‑parenting Zoom with the mediator, and alternating weekend pickups at Maya’s mom’s place in the Third Ward.

No wonder I feel cooked half the time.

From: jordan.a.miller.hou@gmail.com
To: erica.m.miller@nursing.edu
Subject: Money stuff & why I’m so intense about your budget

Hey sis,

I’ve been thinking about that budgeting session we did at my place on W 24th last week and wanted to write some of it down for you.

Seeing your credit report in November 2020 brought back a lot. Your FICO dipping into the low 500s because of the old Capital One auto loan lates and that 2018 charged‑off card is fixable, but only if we stick to the three‑month plan to knock out the $2,300‑plus you’re behind. That’s why I had you list every shift, every clinical, and every dollar.

Part of why I push so hard is what I’ve watched with Mom and Dad. Back when I was at Bayou City Federal Credit Union, I had Mom sitting across from me at the Richmond branch in June 2017 with more than $750 in overdraft fees and a foreclosure notice on the Third Ward house. We set up auto‑transfers around her disability checks, but it was scary seeing it that close.

Same with Dad at the Heights branch on Yale in October 2015, when I had to freeze his joint account after those $600–$900 Friday night ATM withdrawals near that game room on Airline. He admitted to me he was using his Social Security checks for sports bets, and I had to document it and file a SAR with Maria Sanchez in compliance. Treating your own father like any other member is not a fun day at work.

So when I get on you about avoiding overdrafts and payday places, it’s not judgment. I just don’t want you repeating our parents’ stress. Text me when you get your next paycheck and we’ll adjust the plan if we need to.

Love,
Jordan

metadata.subject: Money stuff & why I’m so intense about your budget

artifacts.content:
Title: Maya – therapies, neurology, and speech (log)
Date created: 2024-01-30

Recurring events / notes:

1) Speech therapy – Dr. Alicia Nguyen
• When: Every Tuesday, 3:30–4:15 p.m.
• Where: Texas Children’s Hospital, Medical Center – speech clinic
• Travel block: 2:45–5:00 p.m. from Heights (leave apartment by 2:30 if traffic is bad)
• Notes: HR at First Horizon said in the 1/29/2024 meeting they can’t keep letting me leave the Westheimer branch at 2:45 without reducing hours. Need backup plan with Danielle or Mom on weeks I can’t get away.

2) Neurology – Dr. Karen Lopez
• When: As needed, usually mornings since seizures started March 2016
• Where: Texas Children’s, neurology floor
• Notes: Remember why I left the rotating nights at Target on San Felipe in July 2016 – kept missing these appointments and insurance paperwork.

3) Visitation / co‑parenting logistics
• When: Alternating weekends (2–3 visits/month)
• Where: Pickup at Maya’s mom’s apartment in Third Ward
• Notes: Color‑code this with my Thursday 7 p.m. Wheatley High budgeting workshop and Tuesday 6:30 p.m. Zoom with mediator so I don’t double‑book.

4) My work schedule pressure
• Westheimer branch often schedules 10‑hour shifts; this plus therapy runs is what pushed Dr. Ortiz at Houston Methodist to start me on sertraline in June 2023.

Goal: Keep this calendar updated so Maya’s care (speech, neuro) and court‑ordered time never get sacrificed to branch chaos again.

Thread: Jordan ↔ Mom
Date: 2018-11-21

Jordan: Hey Mom, just checking in before I head to the Westchase branch. How are you feeling?

Mom: Tired but ok baby. How’s work?

Jordan: Long. I’ve got a hardship extension to finish this afternoon for Danielle’s $9,200 personal loan. She fell 60 days past due after maxing two cards and taking that $3,500 payday loan on Bellaire to cover Maya’s NICU bills at Texas Children’s. Feels weird being the one typing all that into the system.

Mom: You always carry everybody’s load. Don’t forget yourself.

Jordan: Hard not to when I keep seeing our own stuff at my desk. I still think about that day at Bayou City FCU on Richmond in June 2017, going through six months of your overdraft fees—over $750—and you handing me the foreclosure notice for the Third Ward house. And before that, Dad at the Heights branch in 2015, admitting those $800 Friday ATM pulls by the game room on Airline were for illegal bets. I had to freeze his account and file a SAR on my own father.

Mom: I know, and I’m sorry you had to be the one.

Jordan: It’s why I’m so intense now about getting out of the high‑pressure places. Between that, Danielle’s loan mess, and how my blood pressure spiked to 180/110 back at Community First’s Galleria branch doing 10‑hour quota shifts before Dr. Howard put me on lisinopril, I’m rethinking what “career” should look like.

Mom: Just don’t lose your heart, baby. That’s what makes you good at this.

Jordan: Trying not to. Love you.

Mom: Love you too.

From: jordan.a.miller.hou@gmail.com
To: patricia.a.miller@example.com
Subject: Why I’m thinking about leaving (again)

Hey Mom,

I know you worry every time I mention changing jobs, so I wanted to lay it all out instead of just saying “work is stressful.”

Back at GulfStar Credit Union’s Heights branch on North Shepherd, that $3,800 over/short incident and the final written warning from Elena Rodriguez in March 2020 pushed me to jump when First Horizon called. Even though internal audit cleared me, the Friday afternoon remedial coaching sessions for three months had me on edge.

Now it feels like a replay. At Westheimer, I’m the one they tap for all the messy stuff—divorces like Jason and Lauren Pierce in June 2023 when I spent an hour in my glass office separating their joint checking and savings per a Harris County temporary order, rerouting her Memorial Hermann paycheck into a new single‑name account so he could freeze the old one. Or the SAR drama with Malik’s Auto Detail between November 2022 and March 2023 after BSA flagged his cash deposits for structuring and we had to close his account. Kevin keeps sending him to me to explain why we filed and why we can’t reopen.

On top of that, every Thursday night I’m at Wheatley High teaching budgeting to juniors and seniors, and in Spring 2023 I was juggling that with the alternating weekends under Maya’s temporary visitation order and the 6:30 p.m. co‑parenting Zoom with the mediator.

I still love the community side—helping folks avoid overdrafts, walking them through credit—but the combination of compliance fires, long shifts, and family court logistics is burning me out. I’m not quitting tomorrow, just starting to look for something with a healthier balance and a stronger community‑development focus, like Unity National or another mission‑driven bank.

Love,
Jordan

Title: Taking care of our parents without losing my mind
Date: 2022-08-19

Sat at the kitchen table tonight after Maya fell asleep and tried to make sense of everything with Mom and Dad.

Yesterday I spent most of the afternoon at the First Horizon Westheimer branch in my office, using my lunch and breaks to handle Mom’s power‑of‑attorney paperwork. I had stacks of Memorial Hermann Greater Heights statements and long‑term care invoices spread across my desk—over $85,000 in bills tied to her diabetes complications and early‑stage dementia workup. I went line by line, organizing due dates, then worked with the back office to set up automatic payments so nothing slips through while she’s foggy.

It felt like déjà vu from other seasons. April 2017, staying late at the Bayou City FCU Heights branch to build Erica’s budget because her checking had gone negative 12 times in three months from covering Dad’s $400 VA copays at the Houston VA Medical Center and the $2,200 past‑due on his First Horizon credit card. I even blocked off a calendar event—“After‑hours session w/ Erica – Dad’s VA copays & $2.2k CC past due” so I wouldn’t forget.

I keep thinking about how all of this shaped my skills: attention to detail from combing through Mom’s $85k in bills, empathy from sitting with members like Angela Carter at the Richmond branch in March 2018 when chemo at MD Anderson had her three months behind on that $18,500 auto loan, and teaching from turning Erica’s real account history into an anonymized example for the budgeting workshop the next week.

But I also need to admit it’s a lot. Note to self: schedule a jog on the trail three mornings this week and maybe one evening just for me and Danielle, no money talk allowed.

metadata.title: Taking care of our parents without losing my mind
